content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_008_page_001.jpg

1. Now concerning food offered to idols: we know that "all of us possess knowledge." This "knowledge" puffs up, but love builds up.

2. If anyone imagines that he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know.

3. But if anyone loves God, he is known by God.

4. Therefore, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that "an idol has no real existence," and that "there is no God but one."

5. For although there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth — as indeed there are many "gods" and many "lords" —

6. yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.

7. However, not all possess this knowledge. But some, through former association with idols, eat food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.

8. Food will not commend us to God. We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.

9. But take care that this right of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.

10. For if anyone sees you who have knowledge eating in an idol's temple, will he not be encouraged, if his conscience is weak, to eat food offered to idols?

11. And so by your knowledge this weak person is destroyed, the brother for whom Christ died.

12. Thus, sinning against your brothers and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.

13. Therefore, if food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 8 introduces the idol-food controversy that extends through chapter 10. Paul establishes a principle that governs all three chapters: love limits liberty.

**Verse 1 — Knowledge vs. love:** EMET identifies the Corinthian slogan. SOPHIA affirms: "knowledge puffs up, love builds up" is the hermeneutical key to chapters 8-10. The Corinthians' knowledge was correct (idols are nothing), but knowledge without love is destructive. φυσιοῖ ("puffs up") is Paul's recurring diagnosis of the Corinthian disease (4:6, 18, 19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4). The cure is not less knowledge but more love.

**Verse 6 — The christological Shema:** EMET traces the reformulation. LOGOS and PNEUMA highlight its theological significance. SOPHIA affirms: this verse is one of the most important christological statements in the Pauline corpus. Paul takes the foundational Jewish confession (Deuteronomy 6:4) and includes Christ within it. The Father is the ultimate source (ἐξ οὗ), Christ is the creative and sustaining agent (δι᾽ οὗ). This is not bitheism — it is a christological expansion of Jewish monotheism. Paul does not add a second God; he identifies Christ as the one through whom the one God creates and sustains all things. This formula, embedded in a discussion about meat, carries the weight of the entire Christian doctrine of God.

**Verses 9-12 — The stumbling block principle:** PNEUMA captures the practical force. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's argument moves from theology to ethics. The strong are theologically right but ethically wrong. Their exercise of liberty in full view of weaker believers (specifically, dining in idol temples) is not neutral — it emboldens others to act against their own conscience. And violating conscience, even an immature conscience, is destructive. The principle is clear: your freedom ends where your brother's destruction begins.

**Verse 11 — "The brother for whom Christ died":** LOGOS identifies this as the decisive theological argument. SOPHIA affirms: Paul weighs the "strong" believer's right to eat against the price Christ paid for the "weak" believer's life. There is no contest. Christ's death for a person infinitely outweighs any individual's right to exercise theological liberty. To destroy someone Christ died to save is to work against Christ — it is, Paul says bluntly, to "sin against Christ" (v.12).

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
